active.description: Flags rows belonging to a duplicated combination of columns expected to together form a unique (composite) business key. The specific columns/table are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Duplicate Composite Key
active.reason: Composite key uniqueness monitoring is cheap insurance for a transactional table.

active.description: Flags duplicate non-null values in a column expected to be unique (e.g. a primary/business key). The specific column/table and why it matters are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Uniqueness Violation
active.reason: ORDER_ID must remain unique; distinct=25 over 25 rows currently confirms it but should be enforced.

active.description: Flags rows where a column value does not match a required regular-expression pattern (phone number, code format, etc.). The specific column/table/pattern are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Pattern Mismatch
active.reason: Pattern checks remain useful for CURRENCY code formatting validation.

active.description: Flags rows where a column expected to hold a strictly positive measure (price, quantity, amount) is zero or negative. The specific column/table and why it matters are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Non-Positive Value
active.reason: AMOUNT should be strictly positive and this check guards against zero/negative money values.

active.description: Flags rows whose column value falls outside an explicit accepted-values list grounded in the column's actually-observed data. The specific column/table/accepted values are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Value Outside Accepted Set
active.reason: STATUS and CURRENCY both have suspect values worth constraining to accepted sets.

active.description: Flags rows whose foreign-key-shaped column has no matching row in the referenced table/column. The specific tables/columns and observed orphan rate are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Referential Integrity Violation
active.reason: CUSTOMER_ID is a foreign-key shaped column that should reference a customers table.

active.description: Flags rows where a column expected to always be populated is NULL. The specific column/table and why it matters are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Not-Null Constraint Violation
active.reason: STATUS, AMOUNT, and CURRENCY all have nulls that should be monitored.

active.description: Flags a table whose most recent timestamp/date column value is older than an expected freshness threshold. The specific column/table/threshold are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Stale Data / Freshness Violation
active.reason: ORDER_DATE freshness matters for an ongoing transactional feed.

active.description: Production tables should track when rows were inserted via a column such as CREATED_AT, CREATE_DATE, or INSERT_TS. This enables auditing, incremental loads, and change tracking.
active.name: Missing Row Creation Timestamp
active.reason: No CREATED_AT column exists; tracking row insertion time is valuable for a fact table.

active.description: Primary key and identifier columns should never be NULL. A nullable PK column breaks referential integrity and causes unexpected results in GROUP BY, JOIN, and deduplication.
active.name: Nullable ID / Primary Key Column
active.reason: ORDER_ID is nullable in the schema and should be flagged as a PK integrity risk.

active.description: Mutable tables should track the last modification time via UPDATED_AT, MODIFIED_DATE, or equivalent. Required for CDC, incremental ETL, and auditing.
active.name: Missing Row Updated Timestamp
active.reason: No UPDATED_AT column exists; order status changes make row modification tracking relevant.

active.description: Columns ending in '_ID' typically reference another table. Add an unenforced REFERENCES clause for documentation and data lineage tools.
active.name: Foreign Key Column Without FK Constraint
active.reason: CUSTOMER_ID ends in _ID and lacks a documented FK constraint.

active.description: Columns should have descriptive comments explaining their data
active.name: Missing Column Comment
active.reason: Columns have no comments; documentation improves usability.

active.description: Tables should have descriptive comments explaining their purpose
active.name: Missing Table Comment
active.reason: Table comment documentation is still worth verifying.

active.description: Flags rows whose column value falls outside an explicit accepted-values list grounded in the column's actually-observed data. The specific column/table/accepted values are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Value Outside Accepted Set
active.reason: Order status drives downstream fulfillment logic; placeholder or unexpected values corrupt reporting on order lifecycle.
active.violation_evidence: STATUS has distinct=8 with a placeholder value 'N/A'(1) plus 4% nulls, exceeding the four legitimate order states.

active.description: Flags rows whose column value falls outside an explicit accepted-values list grounded in the column's actually-observed data. The specific column/table/accepted values are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Value Outside Accepted Set
active.reason: Currency codes must be valid ISO values so that amounts can be correctly converted and aggregated across orders.
active.violation_evidence: CURRENCY has distinct=10 but only 5 clean codes are visible in top_values; extra distinct values and 4% nulls suggest invalid or lowercase/typo codes.

active.description: Flags rows where a column expected to hold a strictly positive measure (price, quantity, amount) is zero or negative. The specific column/table and why it matters are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Non-Positive Value
active.reason: AMOUNT is a monetary measure that must be strictly positive; enforcing this catches free/negative orders resulting from data errors.

active.description: Flags rows where a percentage column falls outside the valid 0-100 range. Discount percentages below 0 or above 100 are logically invalid and distort net revenue calculations.
active.name: Percentage Out of Valid Range
active.reason: A negative or >100% discount produces impossible net amounts and directly corrupts revenue reporting for this fact table.

active.description: Flags rows whose foreign-key-shaped column has no matching row in the referenced table/column. The specific tables/columns and observed orphan rate are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Referential Integrity Violation
active.reason: CUSTOMER_ID is a foreign key; orphaned orders with no matching customer break joins and inflate/undercount customer-level metrics.

skipped.description: Flags rows where a column expected to hold an email address does not match a valid email pattern. The specific column/table is recorded on each instance.
skipped.name: Invalid Email Format
skipped.reason: No email column exists in this table, so this check has no valid target.

skipped.description: Tables should have an assigned owner for accountability
skipped.name: Missing Table Owner
skipped.reason: Table already has an owner (PLAYGROUND_FR_ENGR_ROLE), so this check is not applicable.

skipped.description: Columns ending in _DATE, _DT, or _DAY should use DATE or TIMESTAMP. Storing them as other types prevents date arithmetic and proper sorting.
skipped.name: Column Type Mismatch — Date column should be DATE or TIMESTAMP
skipped.reason: ORDER_DATE is already typed DATE, so no type mismatch exists.

skipped.description: Columns whose names suggest a boolean or flag value (_FL, _FLAG, _IND, IS_, _YN) are stored as VARCHAR. This allows invalid values and prevents efficient filtering. Use BOOLEAN or a small integer type.
skipped.name: Boolean/Flag Column Stored as VARCHAR
skipped.reason: No boolean/flag-named columns exist in this table.

skipped.description: Columns whose names suggest personally identifiable information (e.g. EMAIL, SSN, PHONE, PASSWORD, DOB, SALARY) should have a Snowflake Dynamic Data Masking policy applied and a PII tag attached.
skipped.name: Potential PII Column Without Masking Policy
skipped.reason: No PII-named columns present in this schema.

skipped.description: Columns whose names suggest a date or timestamp are stored as VARCHAR. This prevents date arithmetic, sorting, filtering, and indexing. Convert to DATE or TIMESTAMP.
skipped.name: Date/Timestamp Column Stored as VARCHAR
skipped.reason: ORDER_DATE is stored as DATE, not VARCHAR, so this cannot fire.

skipped.description: Tables with more than 50 columns often indicate poor normalisation, merged business entities, or accumulated technical debt. Consider decomposing into focused, related tables.
skipped.name: Table Has Too Many Columns
skipped.reason: Only 7 columns exist, far below the 50-column threshold.

skipped.description: Columns ending in _ID, _KEY, _FK, or _PK should use a numeric type (NUMBER, INTEGER, BIGINT). Storing them as VARCHAR causes implicit conversions and silent join failures.
skipped.name: Column Type Mismatch — ID/key column should be numeric
skipped.reason: ORDER_ID and CUSTOMER_ID are already NUMBER, satisfying the numeric-ID requirement.

skipped.description: Column names like COL1, DATA, VALUE, FIELD, or MISC provide no semantic context. Rename them to describe what they actually store.
skipped.name: Generic / Uninformative Column Name
skipped.reason: All column names are semantically descriptive; none are generic like COL1/DATA/VALUE.

skipped.description: No column matching common primary-key naming patterns (ID, *_ID, PK_*, *_PK, *_KEY, *_SEQ) was found. Tables without a primary key risk duplicate rows and make joins, deduplication, and CDC harder.
skipped.name: Table May Be Missing a Primary Key
skipped.reason: ORDER_ID matches the primary-key naming pattern, so the table is not missing a PK candidate.

skipped.description: Column names should follow a single naming convention throughout a table (e.g. all UPPER_SNAKE_CASE). Mixing styles makes queries harder to write and datasets harder to join.
skipped.name: Inconsistent Column Naming Style
skipped.reason: All columns already follow consistent UPPER_SNAKE_CASE convention.

output.table_type_reason: ORDERS holds transactional records with measures (AMOUNT, DISCOUNT_PCT), foreign keys (CUSTOMER_ID), and an event date (ORDER_DATE), which is characteristic of a fact table.
